commits.message:
mit-story-5rm.4: thread marks ARE the explode map's chips — one source, loud on a gap

Two review findings, both valid:

[LAW:one-source-of-truth] the thread's five movement families were a second copy
of the list already authored in TREE.layers (the explode 'root' map's chips).
Derive the marks straight from TREE.layers so the thread's marks ARE those chips
— a movement's family or order now changes in exactly one place, and the thread
can't drift from the map it mirrors. This is also the intended relationship: the
explode map previews the arc; the thread is that arc as you live it.

[LAW:no-silent-failure] the mark anchor used `firstOf(fam) ? n.top : closeTop`,
silently re-anchoring a movement with no matching node onto the close — a wrong
map drawn with no signal. Throw loud instead when firstOf returns nothing,
mirroring buildNode's accent guard, so an arc gap surfaces at load.

Verified in live Chrome: marks resolve to the identical five fracs/colours as
before (now derived), zero console errors over a 20-step sweep.

mit-story-5rm.4: the thread — a line that descends with you, gathered by the iris at the close

The signature move: a persistent spine at the left margin that draws downward
as you descend, carrying five family-tinted you-are-here marks (the five
movements the explode map names: the pin / the step / the forms / the data /
the words, in their own families azure·teal·jade·amber·ember). At the close the
converge iris gathers it — the five marks fly to the focal point as the aperture
stops down, so the line you trailed all the way down is what resolves at the
centre. The converge stops being decoration and becomes the plot landing.

A fourth seam part in the now-established shape: a self-contained `thread` with
measure/progress/paint, folded into the page's ONE measure() pass and called
ONCE in tick() off the shared scrollY read — never special-cased into the
generic node loop [LAW:single-enforcer][LAW:decomposition]. Its whole geometry
is DERIVED from the node cache the loop already owns (n.top/n.h): the five marks
sit at the real document positions of the five family sections, and the close
window is the converge node's own seat — it mints no parallel geometry
[LAW:one-source-of-truth]. Families and the arc gradient resolve through
accentOf/--acc-*, so a palette rename breaks loud; the mark ring reuses the
canonical --field-floor token.

progress(y) returns {hp, gp} — head descent across [firstNode.top → convergeTop]
and gather across the converge seat (mirroring the iris's t=clamp(p/0.9)); the
part owns its own progress shape while the tick call stays one uniform
paint(progress(y)) [LAW:dataflow-not-control-flow]. The whole gather transform
(flight + shrink) is motion-gated like the outro; under reduced motion the marks
brighten and snuff in place — state, no flight — so the close still reads.
No layout shift (fixed, pointer-events:none). 360px ADAPTS rather than hides:
the gutter narrows and the marks shrink (the spine is label-free at every width).

Verified in live Chrome: spine draws over a full read-through, marks light in
arc order, the iris receives the gather as it seats, reduced-motion is opacity
state only, 360px adapts, zero console errors over a 24-step sweep.

mit-story-5rm.3: the surfacing — the epilogue rises out of the descent's dark back into paper (#47)

* mit-story-5rm.3: the surfacing — the epilogue rises out of the descent's dark back into paper, mirroring the hero

The descent↔epilogue seam was a hard cut; the entry threshold dramatized
its mirror seam but the exit had no counterpart. Add a 'surfacing' part —
the inverse of 'threshold' — that paints the descent field's floor-dark
(#100e16) as a veil over the epilogue, thinning from full at the rise to
nothing at pin, so the window gives way back to paper and the closing words
resolve into legibility as the page surfaces. [LAW:decomposition] it is its
own seam part obeying the painter contract (measure/progress/paint) WITHOUT
being a node, composed into the single tick() loop by ONE uniform call off
the shared scrollY read — never special-cased into the generic node loop.
[LAW:single-enforcer] it owns ONLY the section background veil; the back-to-
top link's opacity stays the outro's (its exit), so the link surfaces purely
because the veil thins behind it. [LAW:dataflow-not-control-flow] pure
position-driven colour, no transform, no autonomous animation, no
REDUCE_MOTION branch — reduced-motion-safe by construction.

The back-to-top is now the loop made visible: a vertical reflection of the
hero's scroll hint — line-rising→arrow↑→word against the hero's
word→arrow↓→line-dropping — so the first and last frames read as a
deliberate pair, and #top literally re-enters the inception.

Verified live in Chrome: veil alpha 1.0→0.5→0.0 across the rise (smoothstep),
continuous with the dark descent above, pristine paper at pin; zero console
errors.

* mit-story-5rm.3: give the descent floor-dark one home — a --field-floor token

[LAW:one-source-of-truth] the floor-dark #100e16 was the one dark-field value
that had escaped the file's own ':root' tokenisation principle ("every hex
lives once, here"): it was inlined across every scene gradient AND hardcoded
in the threshold's JS veil, and the new surfacing veil copied that JS literal —
a third home for one fact. Promote it to a canonical --field-floor custom
property at :root; point the scene gradients at var(--field-floor); and route
BOTH seam veils through color-mix(in srgb, var(--field-floor) N%, transparent),
the codebase's existing color-mix idiom, so the JS painters name only an alpha
and never re-state the colour. One declaration, derived everywhere.

Verified live in Chrome: --field-floor resolves to #100e16; both veils render
color(srgb 0.0627 0.0549 0.0863 / a) === rgba(16,14,22,a), pixel-identical to
the prior literals; scene gradients resolve var(--field-floor) to rgb(16,14,22);
zero console errors.
